perturbation.original_text: 1.138 "Regulatory Exclusivity" means, with respect to any country or other jurisdiction in the Territory, an additional market protection, other than Patent protection, granted by a Regulatory Authority in such country or other jurisdiction which confers an exclusive Commercialization period during which AbbVie or its Affiliates or Sublicensees has the exclusive right to market and sell, and any unauthorized Third Party is prevented from marketing or selling, a Licensed Compound or Licensed Product in such country or other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.138 "Regulatory Exclusivity" means, with respect to any country or other jurisdiction in the Territory, a potential market advantage, not necessarily guaranteed, granted by a Regulatory Authority in such country or other jurisdiction which may or may not confer an exclusive Commercialization period during which AbbVie or its Affiliates or Sublicensees has the exclusive right to market and sell, and an unauthorized Third Party might still be able to market or sell a Licensed Compound or Licensed Product in such country or other jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of "Regulatory Exclusivity" contradicts the standard understanding and legal implications of regulatory exclusivity, which provides a defined period of market protection. The original text accurately reflects the intended meaning and legal weight of regulatory exclusivity, while the modified text introduces uncertainty and suggests that such exclusivity is not guaranteed, weakening AbbVie’s market position.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FD&C Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the FD&C Act, which provides for specific periods of market exclusivity for certain drugs, preventing generic competition. By suggesting regulatory exclusivity is not guaranteed, the modified text undermines the legal protections provided by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: U.S.C. Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS 21 U.S.C. United States Code, 2021 Edition Title 21 - FOOD AND DRUGS CHAPTER 9 - FEDERAL FOOD, DRUG, AND COSMETIC ACT SUBCHAPTER V - DRUGS AND DEVICES Part A - Drugs and Devices Sec. 355 - New drugs From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §355. New drugs (a) Necessity of effective approval of application No person shall introduce or deliver for introduction into interstate commerce any new drug, unless an approval of an application filed pursuant to subsection (b) or (j) is effective with respect to such drug. (b) Filing application; contents (1)(A) Any person may file with the Secretary an application with respect to any drug subject to the provisions of subsection (a). Such persons shall submit to the Secretary as part of the application— (i) full reports of investigations which have been made to show whether such drug is safe for use and whether such drug is effective in use; (ii) a full list of the articles used as components of such drug; (iii) a full statement of the composition of such drug; (iv) a full description of the methods used in, and the facilities and controls used for, the manufacture, processing, and packing of such drug; (v) such samples of such drug and of the articles used as components thereof as the Secretary may require; (vi) specimens of the labeling proposed to be used for such drug; (vii) any assessments required under section 355c of this title; and (viii) the patent number and expiration date of each patent for which a claim of patent infringement could reasonably be asserted if a person not licensed by the owner of the patent engaged in the manufacture, use, or sale of the drug, and that— (I) claims the drug for which the applicant submitted the application and is a drug sub stance (active ingredient) patent or a drug product (formulation or composition) patent; or (II) claims a method of using such drug for which approval is sought or has been granted in the application. (B) If an application is filed under this subsection for a drug, and a patent of the type described in subparagraph (A)(viii) is issued after the filing date but before approval of the application, the applicant shall amend the applic

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the established legal protections afforded by regulatory exclusivity, as described in the Law Explanation. While the provided snippets do not explicitly define 'regulatory exclusivity,' they reference the FD&C Act (21 U.S.C. § 355) which governs drug approvals and implicitly supports market exclusivity. The change from "exclusive Commercialization period" to a potential, non-guaranteed "market advantage" weakens AbbVie's market position and undermines the legal implications of regulatory exclusivity, therefore creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.108 "Net Sales" means[***] (a) [***] (b) [***] (c) [***] (d) [***] (e) [***] (f) [***] of such Licensed Product and to the extent [***]
perturbation.changed_text: 1.108 "Net Sales" means[***] (a) [***] (b) [***] (c) [***] (d) [***] (e) [***] (f) [***] of such Licensed Product and to the extent [***]. Net Sales will exclude all sales to patients receiving discounts or financial assistance.
perturbation.explanation: The modification adds an explicit exclusion of sales to patients receiving discounts or financial assistance. This is problematic because, while it seems benevolent, it could be interpreted as violating the Anti-Kickback Statute if these discounts are structured improperly.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Anti-Kickback Statute prohibits offering or paying any remuneration to induce referrals of services or items covered by federal healthcare programs. Although the intent of the modification might be charitable, such a broad exclusion from "Net Sales" could be seen as incentivizing prescriptions for certain patients, thus potentially violating the statute.

Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs (a) Making or causing to be made false statements or representations Whoever- (1) knowingly and willfu
perturbation.justification: The modification to the contract introduces a potential violation of the Anti-Kickback Statute, as explained by the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. By excluding "Net Sales" for patients receiving discounts or financial assistance, the contract could be interpreted as offering remuneration (discounts) to induce referrals of services or items covered by federal healthcare programs. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (42 USC 1320a-7b) outlines criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs, which supports the concern that this modification could lead to non-compliance. The changed text alters the definition of 'Net Sales' in a way that could incentivize prescriptions, creating a legally problematic contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 11.5 Special, Indirect, and Other Losses. EXCEPT (A) FOR FRAUD, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR GROSS NEGLIGENCE, (B) FOR A PARTY'S BREACH OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER [ARTICLE 9 OR SECTION 5.8], (C) AS PROVIDED UNDER [***] AND (D) TO THE EXTENT ANY SUCH DAMAGES ARE REQUIRED TO BE PAID TO A THIRD PARTY AS PART OF A CLAIM FOR WHICH A PARTY PROVIDES INDEMNIFICATION UNDER THIS ARTICLE 11, NEITHER PARTY NOR ANY OF ITS AFFILIATES SHALL BE LIABLE FOR INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES, INCLUDING LOSS OF PROFITS OR BUSINESS INTERRUPTION, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, NEGLIGENCE, BREACH OF STATUTORY DUTY OR OTHERWISE IN CONNECTION WITH OR ARISING IN ANY WAY OUT OF THE TERMS OF THIS AGREEMENT OR THE TRANSACTIONS CONTEMPLATED HEREBY OR THE USE OF THE LICENSED COMPOUNDS OR LICENSED PRODUCTS, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.5 Special, Indirect, and Other Losses. EXCEPT (A) FOR FRAUD, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT OR GROSS NEGLIGENCE, (B) FOR A PARTY'S BREACH OF ITS OBLIGATIONS UNDER [ARTICLE 9 OR SECTION 5.8], (C) AS PROVIDED UNDER [***] AND (D) TO THE EXTENT ANY SUCH DAMAGES ARE REQUIRED TO BE PAID TO A THIRD PARTY AS PART OF A CLAIM FOR WHICH A PARTY PROVIDES INDEMNIFICATION UNDER THIS ARTICLE 11, NEITHER PARTY NOR ANY OF ITS AFFILIATES SHALL BE LIABLE FOR INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, PUNITIVE OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT, NEGLIGENCE, BREACH OF STATUTORY DUTY OR OTHERWISE IN CONNECTION WITH OR ARISING IN ANY WAY OUT OF THE TERMS OF THIS AGREEMENT OR THE TRANSACTIONS CONTEMPLATED HEREBY OR THE USE OF THE LICENSED COMPOUNDS OR LICENSED PRODUCTS, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES. Loss of profits or business interruption shall be recoverable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text has a blanket waiver of liability for certain damages. Modifying it to allow for the recovery of 'loss of profits or business interruption' creates conflict and uncertainty. While seemingly straightforward, this change contradicts established case law that often limits the recovery of such damages, especially consequential losses, depending on foreseeability and direct causation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-715 defines a buyer’s incidental and consequential damages. While it acknowledges their possibility, it implies limitations on their recoverability based on factors like foreseeability and causation. The changed text, by stating 'Loss of profits or business interruption shall be recoverable,' removes these limitations and creates potential conflict with the UCC and general contract law principles, which may not allow the recovery of such damages without proof of causation and foreseeability.

perturbation.justification: The original text contains a waiver of liability for indirect, incidental, special, exemplary, punitive or consequential damages, including loss of profits or business interruption. The changed text removes the waiver for 'loss of profits or business interruption.' The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-715 implies limitations on the recoverability of incidental and consequential damages based on factors like foreseeability and causation. By stating 'Loss of profits or business interruption shall be recoverable,' the changed text removes these limitations, potentially conflicting with UCC and general contract law principles. The change creates a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, as contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
